_work.title: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
_work.creator: various
_work.rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
_work.blurb: The deeper figural machinery of astrology beyond the twelve signs: the Children of the Planets cycles in which each planet rules a temperament and a class of trades; the planetary deities riding their chariots; the 36 decans and the 28 mansions of the Moon; and the great fresco programmes of Renaissance Italy — the Salone dei Mesi at Palazzo Schifanoia in Ferrara, the astrological hall of the Salone in Padua — that fused decan figures, planet-rulers, and the labours of the months into a single image of cosmic government.

entries.title: The Planet Saturn and His Children (De zeven planeten series)
entries.work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
entries.creator: Netherlandish printmaker (after the Children of the Planets tradition)
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
**Saturn** riding above the trades and people he governs, from a printed Seven Planets series in the Rijksmuseum, the early-modern descendant of the medieval Children of the Planets cycle.

## Iconography
The aged, scythe-bearing planet rides his **chariot** drawn by the appropriate beasts across the upper register, the zodiac signs of his rulership (Capricorn and Aquarius) beside him. Below, his 'children' pursue the saturnine occupations — labourers, diggers, tanners, cripples, prisoners, and the poor — the lowly, cold, and melancholic trades.

## Significance
The [[motif:children of the planets|Children of the Planets]] schema holds that each planet shapes a temperament and a class of human work. Saturn, slowest and coldest, governs old age, agriculture, mining, and the [[motif:saturn|melancholic]] — a doctrine analysed in Klibansky, Panofsky & Saxl's *Saturn and Melancholy*. The printed series fixed for a broad public the link between planet and social type.

## For artists & game designers
The planet-in-chariot-above, children-below composition is a clean two-register layout for showing a ruling power and the lives it shapes.

citations.text: R. Klibansky, E. Panofsky & F. Saxl, *Saturn and Melancholy* (1964).

entries.title: The Planet Jupiter and His Children (De zeven planeten series)
entries.work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
entries.creator: Netherlandish printmaker (after the Children of the Planets tradition)
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
**Jupiter** presiding over his children from a printed Seven Planets series in the Rijksmuseum, part of the Children of the Planets tradition.

## Iconography
The benign greater fortune rides his **chariot** above, his rulership signs (Sagittarius and Pisces) at hand. Below, his children pursue the dignified, prosperous occupations Jupiter governs — judges, scholars, clergy, hunters, and men of law and government — the well-born and fortunate.

## Significance
In the [[motif:children of the planets|Children of the Planets]] doctrine, Jupiter is the **greater benefic**: he confers honour, piety, justice, and worldly success, and rules the sanguine temperament. Where Saturn's children labour and suffer, Jupiter's prosper. The contrast between the two planets structures the moral geography of the whole series.

## For artists & game designers
Jupiter's register of judges, priests and nobles is a ready 'faction of the fortunate' — the lawful, prosperous counterpart to Saturn's underclass.

entries.title: The Planet Mars and His Children (De zeven planeten series)
entries.work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
entries.creator: Netherlandish printmaker (after the Children of the Planets tradition)
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
**Mars** above a scene of his violent children from a printed Seven Planets series in the Rijksmuseum, part of the Children of the Planets tradition.

## Iconography
The armed war-god rides his **chariot** over a landscape of his works, the signs of his rulership (Aries and Scorpio) beside him. Below, his children make war, burn, rob, and butcher — soldiers, executioners, brigands, and smiths at the forge — the violent, choleric, and iron-working trades.

## Significance
Mars, the **lesser malefic**, governs war, fire, surgery, butchery, and the choleric temperament in the [[motif:children of the planets|Children of the Planets]] scheme. The image of the planet presiding over arson and slaughter visualizes astrological determinism at its harshest: born under Mars, one inherits a life of iron and blood.

## For artists & game designers
Mars's burning, pillaging register is the canonical 'war faction' tableau — soldiers, smiths, and executioners under one red banner.

entries.title: The Planet Venus and Her Children (De zeven planeten series)
entries.work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
entries.creator: Netherlandish printmaker (after the Children of the Planets tradition)
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
**Venus** above her amorous children from a printed Seven Planets series in the Rijksmuseum, part of the Children of the Planets tradition.

## Iconography
The goddess rides her **chariot** over a garden of pleasure, the signs of her rulership (Taurus and Libra) at hand. Below, her children feast, dance, embrace, bathe, and make music — lovers, musicians, and revellers in a scene of courtship and delight.

## Significance
Venus, the **lesser benefic**, governs love, beauty, music, ornament, and the pleasures of the flesh in the [[motif:children of the planets|Children of the Planets]] scheme. The pleasure-garden register is the most sensuous of the series, the planetary opposite of saturnine toil and martial violence.

## For artists & game designers
Venus's garden of lovers and musicians is a self-contained 'pleasure faction' — feasts, music, and courtship as a planet's domain.

entries.title: The Planet Mercury and His Children (De zeven planeten series)
entries.work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
entries.creator: Netherlandish printmaker (after the Children of the Planets tradition)
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
**Mercury** above his industrious children from a printed Seven Planets series in the Rijksmuseum, part of the Children of the Planets tradition.

## Iconography
The planet of intellect rides his **chariot** over a busy workshop-town, the signs of his rulership (Gemini and Virgo) beside him. Below, his children practise the skilled and learned arts — scholars, clockmakers, painters, sculptors, goldsmiths, organists, scribes, and merchants — the trades of hand and mind.

## Significance
Mercury governs eloquence, learning, craft, commerce, and dexterity in the [[motif:children of the planets|Children of the Planets]] scheme; his temperament is mutable, taking on the quality of whatever planet he joins. His register is the most 'civilized' of the series — the world of the studio, the shop, and the school.

## For artists & game designers
Mercury's town of artisans and scholars is the archetypal 'crafts and guilds faction' — the workshop as a planetary domain.

entries.title: Apollo (Sol) with the constellation Leo (Seven Planets and Constellations series)
entries.work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
entries.creator: Netherlandish printmaker (Seven Planets and Constellations series)
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
**Apollo as Sol**, the Sun, paired with his zodiacal house **Leo**, from a Netherlandish Seven Planets and Constellations engraving series in the Rijksmuseum.

## Iconography
The radiant solar deity is shown with the attributes of [[motif:sol|Sol]] — the blazing face, the sun-disc — set beside the **lion** of Leo, the sign of the Sun's domicile. The print belongs to a series joining each planet to its constellation and its 'children'.

## Significance
The Sun rules royalty, gold, magnanimity, vitality, and the choleric-sanguine in astrological doctrine; Leo is its sole domicile. As Apollo, Sol carries the mythic dignity of the planet-deity into the [[motif:children of the planets|Planetenkinder]] tradition, where the Sun's children are kings, athletes, and men of honour.

## For artists & game designers
A single sheet binding a deity, its sign, and its solar attributes is a tidy 'planet card' template for a celestial-faction or zodiac system.

entries.title: Saturn (Francesco Albani planet series), Rijksmuseum
entries.work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
entries.creator: Engraver after Francesco Albani
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
**Saturn** as a planetary deity, an engraving after a painted series by Francesco Albani, in the Rijksmuseum — a Baroque reception of the planet-rulers.

## Iconography
The aged god is shown with the attributes of [[motif:saturn|Saturn]] — the **scythe** of harvest and time, the brooding aspect of age. As Chronos / Saturn he embodies devouring Time, the cold and dry, and the melancholic temperament.

## Significance
The identification of Saturn with melancholy, age, agriculture, and Time runs from antiquity through the medieval Children-of-the-Planets cycles into Renaissance and Baroque art (the great study is Klibansky, Panofsky & Saxl, *Saturn and Melancholy*). Albani's elegant mythological series shows the planetary king surviving as a living artistic type well past the Middle Ages.

## For artists & game designers
The scythe-bearing elder is the definitive 'lord of time and decay' figure — directly usable for a saturnine boss, deity, or melancholic patron.

citations.text: R. Klibansky, E. Panofsky & F. Saxl, *Saturn and Melancholy* (1964).

entries.title: Sol and his children, De Sphaera (Biblioteca Estense, lat. 209)
entries.work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
entries.creator: Lombard workshop, formerly attributed to Cristoforo de Predis
entries.rights: Public domain. Biblioteca Estense, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The **Sun** with his 'children' from the luxurious *De Sphaera* (Modena, Biblioteca Estense, MS lat. 209), a high point of the Children-of-the-Planets tradition.

## Iconography
[[motif:sol|Sol]], the crowned solar ruler, presides at the top of the page over the people and pursuits he governs — kings, wrestlers and athletes, fencers, and men of honour and display in a contemporary Lombard scene.

## Significance
The *De Sphaera* (c. 1470), associated with the Sforza court of Milan, presents each of the seven planets as a crowned king attended by those born under it. The cycle visualizes astrological determinism in social terms: your planet shaped your character and your craft. It is one of the most sumptuous surviving examples of the genre.

## For artists & game designers
A planet-as-king presiding over a townful of its 'children' is a vivid model for a faction, guild, or origin system keyed to seven celestial archetypes.

citations.text: A. Daneu Lattanzi, *Il De Sphaera estense* (1989).

entries.title: Venus and her children, De Sphaera (Biblioteca Estense, lat. 209, f. 9v)
entries.work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
entries.creator: Lombard workshop, formerly attributed to Cristoforo de Predis
entries.rights: Public domain. Biblioteca Estense, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
**Venus** with her 'children' (f. 9v) from the *De Sphaera* (Modena, Biblioteca Estense, MS lat. 209), among the most beautiful of all Children-of-the-Planets pages.

## Iconography
The goddess presides over a courtly garden of pleasure: her children make music, dance, embrace, and bathe together in a fountain or bathhouse. The scene gathers the lovers, musicians and revellers Venus governs into one ravishing Lombard pleasure-park.

## Significance
In the [[motif:children of the planets|Children of the Planets]] scheme, Venus rules love, beauty, music, and bodily delight. The *De Sphaera* page is celebrated for its tender, sensuous treatment of the planet's domain, the courtly counterpart to the popular printed Planetenkinder.

## For artists & game designers
The Venus pleasure-garden — music, bathing, courtship around a fountain — is a ready scene for a leisure or romance space keyed to a single ruling power.

citations.text: A. Daneu Lattanzi, *Il De Sphaera estense* (1989).

entries.title: Mars and his children, De Sphaera (Biblioteca Estense, lat. 209)
entries.work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
entries.creator: Lombard workshop, formerly attributed to Cristoforo de Predis
entries.rights: Public domain. Biblioteca Estense, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
**Mars** with his 'children' from the *De Sphaera* (Modena, Biblioteca Estense, MS lat. 209), the martial page of the Sforza planet-cycle.

## Iconography
The armed war-god presides over a scene of violence and arms — soldiers fighting, a town put to fire and sword, the trades of iron and blood. His children inherit a life of war, butchery, and the forge.

## Significance
Mars, the **lesser malefic**, governs war, fire, surgery and the choleric in the [[motif:children of the planets|Children of the Planets]] scheme. The *De Sphaera* renders the planet's harsh domain with courtly precision: born under Mars, one's lot is iron. The cycle is among the most sumptuous surviving examples of the genre.

## For artists & game designers
The Mars page is a self-contained 'war faction' tableau — soldiers, sieges, and smiths under one planetary lord.

citations.text: A. Daneu Lattanzi, *Il De Sphaera estense* (1989).

entries.title: Mercury and his children, De Sphaera (Biblioteca Estense, lat. 209)
entries.work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
entries.creator: Lombard workshop, formerly attributed to Cristoforo de Predis
entries.rights: Public domain. Biblioteca Estense, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The planet **Mercury** with his 'children' from the *De Sphaera* (Modena, Biblioteca Estense, MS lat. 209), the most famous page of the cycle.

## Iconography
Mercury presides over the trades and arts he governs — scholars, clockmakers, painters, organists, scribes and merchants busy in a contemporary urban scene. This is the [[motif:children of the planets|Children of the Planets]] schema: each planet rules a temperament and a set of human occupations.

## Significance
The *De Sphaera* (c. 1470), associated with the Sforza court of Milan, presents each planet as a crowned ruler attended by the people born under it. Mercury's page — the world of the studio, the shop, and the school — is the most often reproduced, an emblem of the learned and skilled arts under their planetary patron.

## For artists & game designers
Mercury's town of artisans and scholars is the archetypal 'crafts and guilds faction' — the workshop as a planetary domain.

citations.text: A. Daneu Lattanzi, *Il De Sphaera estense* (1989).

entries.title: Jupiter and his children, De Sphaera (Biblioteca Estense, lat. 209)
entries.work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
entries.creator: Lombard workshop, formerly attributed to Cristoforo de Predis
entries.rights: Public domain. Biblioteca Estense, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
**Jupiter** with his 'children' from the *De Sphaera* (Modena, Biblioteca Estense, MS lat. 209), the page of the greater benefic.

## Iconography
Jupiter presides over the dignified pursuits he governs — judges, scholars, clergy, hunters, and men of law and government, the well-born and fortunate at their honourable occupations.

## Significance
In the [[motif:children of the planets|Children of the Planets]] doctrine, Jupiter is the **greater benefic**, conferring honour, piety, justice, and worldly success. His page in the *De Sphaera* is the courtly, prosperous counterpart to saturnine toil and martial violence — the cycle visualizing astrological determinism as a hierarchy of social fortunes.

## For artists & game designers
Jupiter's register of judges, priests and nobles is a ready 'faction of the fortunate' — the lawful, prosperous order of society under its planet.

citations.text: A. Daneu Lattanzi, *Il De Sphaera estense* (1989).

entries.title: Chronos / Saturn, De Sphaera (Biblioteca Estense, lat. 209)
entries.work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
entries.creator: Lombard workshop, formerly attributed to Cristoforo de Predis
entries.rights: Public domain. Biblioteca Estense, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
**Saturn** as Chronos from the *De Sphaera* (Modena, Biblioteca Estense, MS lat. 209), the planet of age and time in the Sforza cycle.

## Iconography
The aged planetary king is shown with the attributes of [[motif:saturn|Saturn]] — the **scythe** of time and harvest, the heavy melancholic bearing. As Chronos he is devouring Time itself, lord of the slow, the cold, and the saturnine.

## Significance
Saturn, the **greater malefic**, governs old age, agriculture, mining, hardship, and the melancholic temperament in the [[motif:children of the planets|Children of the Planets]] scheme. The *De Sphaera* gives the planet's grim domain its courtly treatment; the linked themes of Saturn, Time, and melancholy are the subject of Klibansky, Panofsky & Saxl's classic study.

## For artists & game designers
The scythe-bearing elder of Saturn is the canonical 'lord of time and decay' — a melancholic patron or boss with a built-in mythology.

citations.text: R. Klibansky, E. Panofsky & F. Saxl, *Saturn and Melancholy* (1964).
A. Daneu Lattanzi, *Il De Sphaera estense* (1989).

entries.title: Allegory of March (Aries), Salone dei Mesi, Palazzo Schifanoia, Ferrara
entries.work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
entries.creator: Francesco del Cossa
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 3.0. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A section of the **March** fresco, with the sign **Aries** and its decans, from Francesco del Cossa's contribution to the *Salone dei Mesi* at the Palazzo Schifanoia, Ferrara — the supreme surviving cycle of astrological decan imagery.

## Iconography
Each month's wall is built in three horizontal bands: a triumph of a pagan deity above, the **zodiac sign** with its three **decan** figures in the middle, and scenes of Este court life below. Here March carries [[motif:aries|Aries]] the ram and the strange, unexplained decan-figures of its three ten-degree faces.

## Significance
The Schifanoia decans descend, through Pietro d'Abano's *Astrolabium* and the *Liber Astrologiae* of Georgius Zothorus (Abu Maʿshar's tradition), from the Egyptian and Indian **paranatellonta** — the figures rising with each ten degrees of the zodiac. Aby Warburg's famous 1912 lecture decoded the middle band as this migrated, half-forgotten decan iconography, founding the modern study of astrological image-transmission.

## For artists & game designers
The three-decan band gives 36 enigmatic 'sub-sign' figures — a ready bestiary of celestial minor powers beneath the twelve signs.

citations.text: Aby Warburg, 'Italian Art and International Astrology in the Palazzo Schifanoia' (1912).
Kristen Lippincott, 'The Frescoes of the Salone dei Mesi,' (PhD thesis, 1987).

entries.title: Allegory of April (Triumph of Venus, Taurus), Salone dei Mesi, Palazzo Schifanoia
entries.work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
entries.creator: Francesco del Cossa
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 3.0. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The **April** wall — the **Triumph of Venus** over the sign **Taurus** — from Francesco del Cossa's frescoes in the *Salone dei Mesi*, Palazzo Schifanoia, Ferrara.

## Iconography
The three-band scheme is fully legible here: Venus rides in triumph above in a garden of lovers (Mars kneeling in her chains), the bull of [[motif:taurus|Taurus]] and its three **decan** figures occupy the middle band, and the Este court takes its pleasures below. The triumphs follow the planet that rules each sign — Venus for Taurus.

## Significance
The April panel is the best-preserved and most studied of the whole cycle, the keystone of Aby Warburg's reconstruction of how Egyptian-Indian [[motif:decans|decan]] figures, transmitted through Arabic and medieval Latin astrology, surfaced in Quattrocento Ferrara. It is the single most important monument of Renaissance astrological iconography.

## For artists & game designers
A full month-wall — ruling deity above, sign-and-decans in the middle, courtly life below — is a complete 'celestial governs the world' tableau in one frame.

citations.text: Aby Warburg, 'Italian Art and International Astrology in the Palazzo Schifanoia' (1912).
Kristen Lippincott, 'The Frescoes of the Salone dei Mesi,' (PhD thesis, 1987).

entries.title: Allegory of July (detail, washerwomen), Salone dei Mesi, Palazzo Schifanoia
entries.work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
entries.creator: Ferrarese workshop (Salone dei Mesi)
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 4.0. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A detail of washerwomen (*lavandaie*) from the lower band of the **July** wall in the *Salone dei Mesi*, Palazzo Schifanoia, Ferrara.

## Iconography
This detail comes from the lowest of the three registers — the band of Este court and civic life that runs beneath the [[motif:decans|decan]] figures and the triumph of each month. Here women launder at a basin, a vignette of everyday labour set under the rule of the month's sign.

## Significance
The lower band grounds the astrological cosmos of the *Salone* in the lived world of Ferrara: the heavens above (deity-triumph and sign-with-decans) govern the human activity below. The cycle thus joins the abstract [[motif:decans|decan]] system to a vivid panorama of fifteenth-century life, a structure Warburg read as the meeting of high astrology and local court culture.

## For artists & game designers
The genre-detail register shows how to anchor a cosmic scheme in ordinary life — celestial rule made concrete in small scenes of work and play.

citations.text: Aby Warburg, 'Italian Art and International Astrology in the Palazzo Schifanoia' (1912).
Kristen Lippincott, 'The Frescoes of the Salone dei Mesi,' (PhD thesis, 1987).

entries.title: Allegory of December (Triumph of Vesta), Salone dei Mesi, Palazzo Schifanoia
entries.work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
entries.creator: Ferrarese workshop (Salone dei Mesi)
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 3.0. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The **December** wall, with its **Triumph of Vesta**, from the *Salone dei Mesi* at the Palazzo Schifanoia, Ferrara. (The Commons filename's '01 dicembre' fixes this as the December, not September, panel.)

## Iconography
The upper band carries the triumph of the deity who rules December's sign — here **Vesta** — above the zodiacal band that would hold [[motif:capricorn|Capricorn]] and its three **decan** figures, with the Este court below. This wall is among the more damaged of the cycle.

## Significance
The *Salone dei Mesi* assigns each month a presiding pagan deity drawn from Manilius's scheme of the tutelary gods of the signs, set over the [[motif:decans|decan]] figures that descend from the Egyptian-Arabic [[motif:paranatellonta|paranatellonta]]. The December panel completes the year's round of triumphs in the great programme Aby Warburg made the founding case-study of astrological iconology.

## For artists & game designers
A triumph-of-a-deity upper register is a clean 'patron god of the month' device for a calendar or seasonal-festival system.

citations.text: Aby Warburg, 'Italian Art and International Astrology in the Palazzo Schifanoia' (1912).
Marco Bertozzi, *La tirannia degli astri: Aby Warburg e l'astrologia di Palazzo Schifanoia* (1985).

entries.title: Astrological hall of the Salone, Palazzo della Ragione, Padua
entries.work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
entries.creator: After Giotto's lost cycle; repainted by Nicolò Miretto and Stefano da Ferrara
entries.rights: CC BY 2.0. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The vast hall of the **Palazzo della Ragione** in Padua, whose walls carry one of the largest medieval-Renaissance astrological fresco cycles in existence.

## Iconography
The enormous **Salone** is frescoed in registers running the length of the hall: the **zodiac signs**, the planets, the months and their labours, the apostles, and the **decan**-related figures of an astrological calendar, organising the year and the heavens around the room.

## Significance
The Paduan cycle derives from a lost programme designed under the influence of **Pietro d'Abano**, the city's great astrologer-physician (d. c. 1316), and originally painted by Giotto's workshop; it was repainted in the fifteenth century after a fire by Nicolò Miretto and Stefano da Ferrara. With the Schifanoia *Salone dei Mesi*, it is a principal monument of the Italian astrological hall, binding [[motif:decans|decan]] and calendar imagery into a single civic cosmos.

## For artists & game designers
An entire room as a walk-through calendar-cosmos is an architectural model for an astrological 'world map' space the player physically moves through.

citations.text: Eva Frojmovič, 'Giotto's Allegories of Justice and the Commune,' *JWCI* (1996).

entries.title: Constellation woodcuts, Hyginus, Poeticon Astronomicon
entries.work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
entries.creator: After Hyginus; early printed edition (Venice tradition)
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A page of constellation woodcuts from an early printed edition of the *Poeticon Astronomicon* attributed to **Hyginus**, the standard classical handbook of the star-myths.

## Iconography
The **constellations** are drawn as classical **personifications** — mythological figures with the stars of each constellation marked upon their bodies. The *Poeticon Astronomicon* gathered the Greek catasterism legends, explaining how heroes and beasts were set among the stars.

## Significance
The text ascribed to Hyginus (Augustan era) was, with Aratus, the great ancient source for the figures of the sky; its richly woodcut Venetian editions (Ratdolt, 1482, and successors) became the visual canon of the constellations for the Renaissance. These figures are also the raw material of the [[motif:paranatellonta|paranatellonta]] — the constellations that rise alongside the zodiac and underlie the decan imagery.

## For artists & game designers
Classical constellation-personifications with stars pricked onto them are a beautiful template for a 'living sky' or star-summoned creature bestiary.

entries.title: Rhetorica (E23), so-called Tarocchi of Mantegna (E-series)
entries.work: The Figural System of Astrology: Decans, Mansions & Planet-Children
entries.creator: Anonymous North Italian engraver ('Master of the E-Series')
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
**Rhetorica** (card E23), one of the fifty engravings of the so-called *Tarocchi of Mantegna* (the E-series), a North Italian cosmological print cycle of c. 1465 — not in fact tarot cards nor by Mantegna.

## Iconography
The sheet personifies **Rhetoric** as an allegorical woman with her attributes, one of the seven Liberal Arts in the cycle. The full series ascends a Neoplatonic ladder from the conditions of men, through Apollo and the Muses, the Liberal Arts and Virtues, the seven **planets**, and the spheres up to the *Prima Causa*.

## Significance
The E-series is a complete diagram of the hierarchical cosmos: its planet-cards (Luna to Saturn, then the Eighth Sphere and Prime Mover) place the [[motif:planetary deities|planetary rulers]] within a graded chain of being. It is a key Renaissance synthesis of astrology, the arts, and Neoplatonic cosmology, hugely influential on later print culture (Dürer copied from it).

## For artists & game designers
A fifty-card ladder from common men to the Prime Mover is a ready 'rank / cosmic tier' system — each step a clean allegorical figure.

citations.text: Mark J. Zucker, *The Illustrated Bartsch 24: Early Italian Masters* (the 'Tarocchi').
Jay A. Levenson et al., *Early Italian Engravings from the National Gallery of Art* (1973).
